[267:30]why are the consumers getting a 34% increase in price?

[267:35]Because Chinese exporters have what is called

[267:38]They have a profit margin.

[267:43]They get to be competitive.

[267:46]They get to have a high profit margin

[267:49]because the Chinese currency is weaker

[267:52]and because the Chinese labor cost is lower.

[268:00]which is strong.

[268:02]And it's a factors of production advantage

[268:04]against the United States, which does not have the capital

[268:07]because of decades of deindustrialization

[268:09]and does not have the labor

[268:10]because it has a smaller and less skilled workforce.

[268:20]that 34% doesn't get passed on because when goods come together, you have profit margin

[268:42]the goods that should have been most affected

[268:44]did not become more expensive,

[268:46]did not undergo upward pressure and price,

[268:56]there were articles about this at the time.

[270:35]or in some cases any cost,

[270:37]and if they're paying any cost, it's a one-time cost,

[270:41]it's because they're not actually paying it.

[270:44]What is a tariff?

[270:45]A tariff is a tax, but the tax is paid at the port.

[270:50]The tax is paid, the tariff is levied on the cost that the importer pays.

[270:57]The importer pays the exporter.

[271:01]The exporter charges the importer, you know, let's say the T-shirt costs $5 to make.

[271:12]that they pay to the exporter, the $5.

[271:15]They pay that tax at the port.

[271:18]When the goods come through the port,

[271:21]it's identified where the good came from,

[271:24]where the country of origin was, what the good is.

[271:27]And then according to the tariff schedule,

[271:28]it is charged a rate.

[271:30]And it is a company that pays the tax.

[271:32]It is a real tax.

[271:33]And the consumer does not pay the real tax.

[271:37]What people are saying,

[271:39]But they're not saying it like this

[271:40]What people mean when they say this is that consumers are indirectly paying the tax

[271:46]That's what they mean when they say that when they say tariffs are a tax on consumers. It's not

[271:54]Consumers don't pay the tax the importer pays the tax the consumer doesn't pay the tax at the cash register at the point of sale

[272:01]The importer pays the tax at the port

[272:06]It's a real tax and that's who's paying it and that's where they're paying it and that's what they're paying it based on

[272:11]So when people say the consumer pays the tax what they mean is that?

[272:15]Indirectly the cost of the tax can be partially passed down to the consumer

[272:21]Why don't they say it that way?

[272:24]The reason they don't say it that way is because it is not necessarily so that an importer

[272:30]has to pay the tariff.

[272:32]Why?

[272:48]is because China has high trade barriers against the United States. So how do you pay no tariffs?

[273:05]practices and intellectual property theft and dumping in a number of other things that they do.

[295:47]who puts the pencil together.

[296:03]for strategic reasons.

[296:49]And these people that say,

[296:51]oh, a current account deficit,

[296:56]because the word deficit is in it,

[296:57]but it's just a matter of accounting.
